steps.title: Ensure Scene Safety
steps.subtitle:
• Make sure you are safe before helping.
• Remove the person from the source of poison (gas, chemicals, fumes).
• Avoid direct contact with unknown substances (use gloves if available).
• If it’s a gas exposure (like carbon monoxide), move to fresh air immediately.

steps.title: Check Responsiveness & ABCs
steps.subtitle:
• A — Airway: Is the airway clear?
• B — Breathing: Is the person breathing normally?
• C — Circulation: Is there a pulse?

If unconscious and not breathing, start CPR and call Emergency Medical Services immediately.

steps.title: Call for Professional Help
steps.subtitle:
• Call your local emergency number right away.
• In the U.S., contact Poison Control (1-800-222-1222).
• Provide:
  • Age and weight of the person
  • Substance involved (bring the container if possible)
  • Amount taken
  • Time of exposure
  • Symptoms
Do not wait for symptoms to appear.

steps.title: Do NOT Induce Vomiting (Unless Directed)
steps.subtitle:
• Do not give food, drink, or milk unless instructed by medical professionals.
• Do not induce vomiting unless poison control specifically tells you to.
• If the poison is on the skin, rinse with running water for 15 minutes.
• If in eyes, flush gently with clean water for 15 minutes.

steps.title: Position the Person Properly
steps.subtitle:
• If unconscious but breathing, place in the recovery position (on their side).
• If vomiting, turn head to the side to prevent choking.
• Keep them calm and still.

steps.title: Monitor & Document
steps.subtitle:
Watch for:
• Difficulty breathing
• Seizures
• Chest pain
• Severe drowsiness
• Bluish lips or nails
• Note any changes in condition.
• Be ready to give updates to emergency responders.

Seek Immediate Emergency Care If:
• The person collapses
• Has trouble breathing
• Has seizures
• Is unconscious
• The poison is a strong acid, alkali, or petroleum product
